step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("lesions caused by HSV in infants")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the extensive evidence gathered, we see strong support for HSV as the causative agent in neonatal presentations involving vesicular eruptions on the face and limbs. The literature confirms that HSV frequently leads to skin lesions manifesting as vesicles, especially in the semigroup (skin, eyes, mouth) configuration mentioned. Furthermore, the high prevalence rate of skin vesicles in neonates with systemic HSV infection underscores why this feature stands out prominently in differentiating HSV from other pathogens like CMV or Coxsackievirus. Given that nearly three-quarters of affected neonates exhibit skin lesions alongside other systemic symptoms, the presence of such lesions makes HSV a very probable candidate. Thus, my agreement with the original conclusion remains firm:
